nativeRange.habitat: Rocky areas, rubble and sand around islands, typically at depths of 3–25 m, most commonly shallower than 10 m

diet.notes: Has been documented consuming algae, sponges, small invertebrates, sea squirts, worms, and seaweed in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer meaty foods such as live or frozen brine shrimp, black worms, and glass shrimp alongside algae-based foods. Constant grazers; feeding multiple small portions daily tends to work better than single large meals. Floating foods should be avoided as ingesting air can cause buoyancy problems.

avoidWith: aggressive fish that may stress the cowfish

imageCandidates.notes: Francesco Cecere, 2048x1536. Live in-situ reef photo clearly showing the diagnostic horn projections over the eye and reticulated honeycomb body pattern with white/yellow spots; sharp on the fish, mild underwater haze in background only.

summary: The Island Cowfish is a rare Eastern Atlantic boxfish reaching around 50 cm, known for secreting ostracitoxin when stressed. Generally considered expert-only due to its toxin risk and large space requirements.
careNotes: The Island Cowfish is one of the more challenging and rarely encountered species in the marine aquarium trade. It tends to be a slow, deliberate swimmer that grazes continuously throughout the day. The most critical husbandry concern is its ability to secrete ostracitoxin (also called pahutoxin), a potent ichthyotoxin released into the water when the fish is stressed, injured, or dying. This toxin has been known to kill other fish — and in some cases invertebrates — in the same system. Running activated carbon is commonly advised as a precaution, and the fish should be removed promptly if it appears to be in poor health or being harassed. For this reason, it is generally advised to introduce this species into the aquarium first, before other inhabitants, and to keep it only with robust, non-aggressive tankmates that will not stress it. The aquarium should provide ample open swimming space as well as live rock for hiding and grazing. Tank size recommendations vary considerably across sources, ranging from around 250 US gallons (approx. 946 L) at the low end to 2000 L or more in specialist guidance; a minimum of 1000 L is a cautious starting point, and a larger system is preferable. Temperature recommendations show some spread as well, with hobby sources typically citing 72–78°F (22–25.6°C) and wild-range data extending the upper end to 28°C; 22–27°C covers the practical middle ground. This species is not considered reef-safe: it tends to pick at invertebrates including sponges and small crustaceans, and the ostracitoxin risk makes it incompatible with reef systems. Acclimating newly arrived specimens to prepared foods can be difficult; live enriched foods such as brine shrimp and glass shrimp are commonly suggested as a bridge to frozen and prepared diets.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (50 cm confirmed), native range, temperature (21.5–28°C), taxonomy (Ostraciidae, Tetraodontiformes), and habitat depth. Temperature max of 28°C is higher than hobby sources; averaged to 27°C.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Confirmed size (~20 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), and 250-gallon minimum tank size. Care level listed as 'Difficult' vs LiveAquaria's 'Expert Only'; used 'expert' as dominant designation.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size of 50 cm. Recommends at least 2000 L minimum tank — substantially larger than the 946 L from retail sources. Tank size disagreement is >2× (946 L vs 2000 L); 1000 L chosen as cautiously rounded compromise. Also confirmed omnivorous diet including sponges, invertebrates, algae, worms.
